## **Volume Rendering of Neural Implicit Surfaces**

**Lior Yariv**[1] **Jiatao Gu**[2] **Yoni Kasten**[1] **Yaron Lipman**[1] _[,]_[2]

1Weizmann Institute of Science 2Facebook AI Research

## **Abstract**

Neural volume rendering became increasingly popular recently due to its success in synthesizing novel views of a scene from a sparse set of input images. So far, the geometry learned by neural volume rendering techniques was modeled using a generic density function. Furthermore, the geometry itself was extracted using an arbitrary level set of the density function leading to a noisy, often low fidelity reconstruction. The goal of this paper is to improve geometry representation and reconstruction in neural volume rendering. We achieve that by modeling the volume density as a function of the geometry. This is in contrast to previous work modeling the geometry as a function of the volume density. In more detail, we define the volume density function as Laplace’s cumulative distribution function (CDF) applied to a signed distance function (SDF) representation. This simple density representation has three benefits: (i) it provides a useful inductive bias to the geometry learned in the neural volume rendering process; (ii) it facilitates a bound on the opacity approximation error, leading to an accurate sampling of the viewing ray. Accurate sampling is important to provide a precise coupling of geometry and radiance; and (iii) it allows efficient unsupervised disentanglement of shape and appearance in volume rendering. Applying this new density representation to challenging scene multiview datasets produced high quality geometry reconstructions, outperforming relevant baselines. Furthermore, switching shape and appearance between scenes is possible due to the disentanglement of the two.

## **1 Introduction**

Volume rendering [18] is a set of techniques that renders volume _density_ in _radiance fields_ by the so called volume rendering integral. It has recently been shown that representing both the density and radiance fields as neural networks can lead to excellent prediction of novel views by learning only from a sparse set of input images. This neural volume rendering approach, presented in [21] and developed by its follow-ups [34, 2] approximates the integral as alpha-composition in a differentiable way, allowing to learn simultaneously both from input images. Although this coupling indeed leads to good generalization of novel viewing directions, the density part is not as successful in faithfully predicting the scene’s actual geometry, often producing noisy, low fidelity geometry approximation.

We propose VolSDF to devise a different model for the density in neural volume rendering, leading to better approximation of the scene’s geometry while maintaining the quality of view synthesis. The key idea is to represent the density as a function of the signed distance to the scene’s surface, see Figure 1. Such density function enjoys several benefits. First, it guarantees the existence of a well-defined surface that generates the density. This provides a useful inductive bias for disentangling density and radiance fields, which in turn provides a more accurate geometry approximation. Second, we show this density formulation allows bounding the approximation error of the opacity along rays. This bound is used to sample the viewing ray so to provide a faithful coupling of density and radiance field in the volume rendering integral. E.g., without such a bound the computed radiance along a ray (pixel color) can potentially miss or extend surface parts leading to incorrect radiance approximation.

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

Figure 1: VolSDF: given a set of input images (left) we learn a volumetric density (center-left, sliced) defined by a signed distance function (center-right, sliced) to produce a neural rendering (right). This definition of density facilitates high quality geometry reconstruction (gray surfaces, middle).

A closely related line of research, often referred to as neural implicit surfaces [22, 38, 14], have been focusing on representing the scene’s geometry implicitly using a neural network, making the surface rendering process differentiable. The main drawback of these methods is their requirement of masks that separate objects from the background. Also, learning to render surfaces directly tends to grow extraneous parts due to optimization problems, which are avoided by volume rendering. In a sense, our work combines the best of both worlds: _volume rendering with neural implicit surfaces_ .

We demonstrate the efficacy of VolSDF by reconstructing surfaces from the DTU [12] and BlendedMVS [37] datasets. VolSDF produces more accurate surface reconstructions compared to NeRF [21] and NeRF++ [39], and comparable reconstruction compared to IDR [38], while avoiding the use of object masks. Furthermore, we show disentanglement results with our method, i.e., switching the density and radiance fields of different scenes, which is shown to fail in NeRF-based models.

## **2 Related work**

**Neural Scene Representation & Rendering** Implicit functions are traditionally adopted in modeling 3D scenes [24, 11, 4]. Recent studies have been focusing on model implicit functions with multi-layer perceptron (MLP) due to its expressive representation power and low memory foot-print, including scene (geometry & appearance) representation [9, 20, 19, 23, 25, 29, 36, 28, 35] and free-view rendering [33, 16, 30, 26, 17, 21, 15, 39, 34, 2]. In particular, NeRF [21] has opened up a line of research (see [6] for an overview) combining neural implicit functions together with volume rendering to achieve photo-realistic rendering results. However, it is non-trivial to find a proper threshold to extract surfaces from the predicted density, and the recovered geometry is far from satisfactory. Furthermore, sampling of points along a ray for rendering a pixel is done using an opacity function that is approximated from another network without any guarantee for correct approximation.

**Multi-view 3D Reconstruction** Image-based 3D surface reconstruction (multi-view stereo) has been a longstanding problem in the past decades. Classical multi-view stereo approaches are generally either depth-based [1, 31, 8, 7] or voxel-based [5, 3, 32]. For instance, in COLMAP [31] (a typical depth-based method) image features are extracted and matched across different views to estimate depth. Then the predicted depth maps are fused to obtain dense point clouds. To obtain the surface, an additional meshing step e.g. Poisson surface reconstruction [13] is applied. However, these methods with complex pipelines may accumulate errors at each stage and usually result in incomplete 3D models, especially for non-Lambertian surfaces as they can not handle view dependent colors. On the contrary, although it produces complete models by directly modeling objects in a volume, voxel-based approaches are limited to low resolution due to high memory consumption. Recently, neural-based approaches such as DVR [22], IDR [38], NLR [14] have also been proposed to reconstruct scene geometry from multi-view images. However, these methods require accurate object masks and appropriate weight initialization due to the difficulty of propagating gradients.

Independently from and concurrently with our work here, [27] also use implicit surface representation incorporated into volume rendering. In particular, they replace the local transparency function with an occupancy network [19]. This allows adding surface smoothing term to the loss, improving the quality of the resulting surfaces. Differently from their approach, we use signed distance representation, regularized with an Eikonal loss [38, 10] without any explicit smoothing term. Furthermore, we show that the choice of using signed distance allows bounding the opacity approximation error, facilitating the approximation of the volume rendering integral for the suggested family of densities.

## **3 Method**

In this section we introduce a novel parameterization for volume density, defined as transformed signed distance function. Then we show how this definition facilitates the volume rendering process. In particular, we derive a bound of the error in the opacity approximation and consequently devise a sampling procedure for approximating the volume rendering integral.

## **3.1 Density as transformed SDF**

Let the set Ω _⊂_ R[3] represent the space occupied by some object in R[3] , and _M_ = _∂_ Ω its boundary surface. We denote by **1** Ω the Ω indicator function, and by _d_ Ω the Signed Distance Function (SDF) to its boundary _M_ ,

where _∥·∥_ is the standard Euclidean 2-norm. In neural volume rendering the volume _density σ_ : R[3] _→_ R+ is a scalar volumetric function, where _σ_ ( _**x**_ ) is the rate that light is occluded at point _**x**_ ; _σ_ is called density since it is proportional to the particle count per unit volume at _**x**_ [18]. In previous neural volumetric rendering approaches [21, 15, 39], the density function, _σ_ , was modeled with a general-purpose Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP). In this work we suggest to model the density using a certain transformation of a learnable Signed Distance Function (SDF) _d_ Ω, namely

_σ_ ( _**x**_ ) = _α_ Ψ _β_ ( _−d_ Ω( _**x**_ )) _,_ (2)

where _α, β >_ 0 are learnable parameters, and Ψ _β_ is the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of the Laplace distribution with zero mean and _β_ scale (i.e., mean absolute deviation, which is intuitively the _L_ 1 version of the standard deviation),

Figure 1 (center left and right) depicts an example of such a density and SDF. As can be readily checked from this definition, as _β_ approach zero, the density _σ_ converges to a scaled indicator function of Ω, that is _σ → α_ **1** Ω for all points _**x** ∈_ Ω _\ M_ .

Intuitively, the density _σ_ models a homogeneous object with a constant density _α_ that smoothly decreases near the object’s boundary, where the smoothing amount is controlled by _β_ . The benefit in defining the density as in equation 2 is two-fold: First, it provides a useful inductive bias for the surface geometry _M_ , and provides a principled way to reconstruct the surface, i.e., as the zero level-set of _d_ Ω. This is in contrast to previous work where the reconstruction was chosen as an arbitrary level set of the learned density. Second, the particular form of the density as defined in equation 2 facilitates a bound on the error of the _opacity_ (or, equivalently the _transparency_ ) of the rendered volume, a crucial component in the volumetric rendering pipeline. In contrast, such a bound will be hard to devise for a generic MLP densities.

## **3.2 Volume rendering of** _σ_

In this section we review the volume rendering integral and the numerical integration commonly used to approximate it, requiring a set _S_ of sample points per ray. In the following section (Section 3.3), we explore the properties of the density _σ_ and derive a bound on the opacity approximation error along viewing rays. Finally, in Section 3.4 we derive an algorithm for producing a sample _S_ to be used in the volume rendering numerical integration.

In volume rendering we consider a ray _**x**_ emanating from a camera position _**c** ∈_ R[3] in direction _**v** ∈_ R[3] , _∥_ _**v** ∥_ = 1, defined by _**x**_ ( _t_ ) = _**c**_ + _t_ _**v**_ , _t ≥_ 0. In essence, volume rendering is all about approximating the integrated (i.e., summed) light radiance along this ray reaching the camera. There are two important quantities that participate in this computation: the volume’s _opacity O_ , or equivalently, its _transperancy T_ , and the _radiance field L_ .

The _transparency_ function of the volume along a ray _**x**_ , denoted _T_ , indicates, for each _t ≥_ 0, the probability a light particle succeeds traversing the segment [ _**c** ,_ _**x**_ ( _t_ )] without bouncing off,

**Sampling.** Since the PDF _τ_ is typically extremely concentrated near the object’s boundary (see e.g., Figure 3, right) the choice of the sample points _S_ has a crucial effect on the approximation quality of equation 8. One solution is to use an adaptive sample, e.g., _S_ computed with the inverse CDF, i.e., _O[−]_[1] . However, _O_ depends on the density model _σ_ and is not given explicitly. In [21] a second, coarse network was trained specifically for the approximation of the opacity _O_ , and was used for inverse sampling. However, the second network’s density does not necessarily faithfully represents the first network’s density, for which we wish to compute the volume integral. Furthermore, as we show later, one level of sampling could be insufficient to produce an accurate sample _S_ . Using a naive or crude approximation of _O_ would lead to a sub-optimal sample set _S_ that misses, or over extends non-negligible _τ_ values. Consequently, incorrect radiance approximations can occur (i.e., pixel color), potentially harming the learned density-radiance field decomposition. Our solution works with a single density _σ_ , and the sampling _S_ is computed by a sampling algorithm based on an error bound for the opacity approximation. ## **3.4 Sampling algorithm**

In this section we develop an algorithm for computing the sampling _S_ to be used in equation 8. This is done by first utilizing the bound in equation 15 to find samples _T_ so that _O_[�] (via equation 10) provides an _ϵ_ approximation to the true opacity _O_ , where _ϵ_ is a hyper-parameter, that is _BT ,β < ϵ_ . Second, we perform inverse CDF sampling with _O_[ˆ] , as described in Section 3.2.

Note that from Lemma 1 it follows that we can simply choose large enough _n_ to ensure _BT ,β < ϵ_ . However, this would lead to prohibitively large number of samples. Instead, we suggest a simple algorithm to reduce the number of required samples in practice and allows working with a limited budget of sample points. In a nutshell, we start with a uniform sampling _T_ = _T_ 0, and use Lemma 2 to initially set a _β_ + _> β_ that satisfies _BT ,β_ + _≤ ϵ_ . Then, we repeatedly upsample _T_ to reduce _β_ + while maintaining _BT ,β_ + _≤ ϵ_ . Even though this simple strategy is not guaranteed to converge, we find that _β_ + usually converges to _β_ (typically 85%, see also Figure 3), and even in cases it does not, the algorithm provides _β_ + for which the opacity approximation still maintains an _ϵ_ error. The algorithm is presented below (Algorithm 1).

We initialize _T_ (Line 1 in Algorithm 1) with uniform sampling _T_ 0 = _{ti}[n] i_ =1[, where] _[ t][k]_[= (] _[k][−]_[1)] _n[M] −_ 1[,] _k ∈_ [ _n_ ] (we use _n_ = 128 in our implementation). Given this sampling we next pick _β_ + _> β_ according to Lemma 2 so that the error bound satisfies the required _ϵ_ bound (Line 2 in Algorithm 1).

In order to reduce _β_ + while keep _BT ,β_ + _≤ ϵ_ , _n_ samples are added to _T_ (Line 4 in Algorithm 1), where the number of points sampled from each interval is proportional to its current error bound, equation 14. Assuming _T_ was sufficiently upsampled and satisfy _BT ,β_ + _< ϵ_ , we decrease _β_ + towards _β_ . Since the algorithm did not stop we have that _BT ,β > ϵ_ . Therefore the Mean Value Theorem implies the existence of _β⋆ ∈_ ( _β, β_ +) such that _BT ,β⋆_ = _ϵ_ . We use the bisection method (with maximum of 10 iterations) to efficiently search for _β⋆_ and update _β_ + accordingly (Lines 6 and 7 in Algorithm 1). The algorithm runs iteratively until _BT ,β ≤ ϵ_ or a maximal number of 5 iterations is reached. Either way, we use the final _T_ and _β_ + (guaranteed to provide _BT ,β_ + _≤ ϵ_ ) to estimate the current opacity _O_[�] , Line 10 in Algorithm 1). Finally we return a fresh set of _m_ = 64 samples _O_[ˆ] using inverse transform sampling (Line 11 in Algorithm 1). ## **3.5 Training**

Our system consists of two Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLP): (i) _**f** ϕ_ approximating the SDF of the learned geometry, as well as global geometry feature _**z**_ of dimension 256, i.e., _**f** ϕ_ ( _**x**_ ) = ( _d_ ( _**x**_ ) _,_ _**z**_ ( _**x**_ )) _∈_ R[1+256] , where _ϕ_ denotes its learnable parameters; (ii) _Lψ_ ( _**x** ,_ _**n** ,_ _**v** ,_ _**z**_ ) _∈_ R[3] representing the scene’s radiance field with learnable parameters _ψ_ . In addition we have two scalar learnable parameters _α, β ∈_ R. In fact, in our implementation we make the choice _α_ = _β[−]_[1] . We denote by _θ ∈_ R _[p]_ the collection of all learnable parameters of the model, _θ_ = ( _ϕ, ψ, β_ ). To facilitate the learning of high frequency details of the geometry and radiance field, we exploit positional encoding [21] for the position _**x**_ and view direction _**v**_ in the geometry and radiance field. The influence of different positional encoding choices are presented in the supplementary.

Our data consists of a collection of images with camera parameters. From this data we extract pixel level data: for each pixel _p_ we have a triplet ( _Ip,_ _**c** p,_ _**v** p_ ), where _Ip ∈_ R[3] is its intensity (RGB color), _**c** p ∈_ R[3] is its camera location, and _**v** p ∈_ R[3] is the viewing direction (camera to pixel). We train with batches of size 1024 pixels _p_ . _λ_ is a hyper-parameter set to 0 _._ 1 throughout the the experiments. Further implementation details are provided in the supplementary.

## **4 Experiments**

We evaluate our method on the challenging task of multiview 3D surface reconstruction. We use two datasets: DTU [12] and BlendedMVS [37], both containing real objects with different materials that are captured from multiple views. In Section 4.1 we show qualitative and quantitative 3D surface reconstruction results of VolSDF, comparing favorably to relevant baselines. In Section 4.2 we demonstrate that, in contrast to NeRF [21], our model is able to successfully disentangle the geometry and appearance of the captured objects.

## **4.1 Multi-view 3D reconstruction**

**DTU** The DTU [12] dataset contains multi-view image (49 or 64) of different objects with fixed camera and lighting parameters. We evaluate our method on the 15 scans that were selected by [38]. We compare our surface accuracy using the Chamfer _l_ 1 loss (measured in mm) to COLMAP0 (which is watertight reconstruction; COLMAP7 is not watertight and provided only for reference) [31], NeRF [21] and IDR [38], where for fair comparison with IDR we only evaluate the reconstruction inside the visual hull of the objects (defined by the segmentation masks of [38]). We further evaluate the PSNR of our rendering compared to [21]. Quantitative results are presented in Table 1. It can be observed that our method is on par with IDR (that uses object masks for all images) and outperforms NeRF and COLMAP in terms of reconstruction accuracy. Our rendering quality is comparable to NeRF’s.

**BlendedMVS** The BlendedMVS dataset [37] contains a large collection of 113 scenes captured from multiple views. It supplies high quality ground truth 3D models for evaluation, various camera configurations, and a variety of indoor/outdoor real environments. We selected 9 different scenes and used our method to reconstruct the surface of each object. In contrast to the DTU dataset, BlendedMVS scenes have complex backgrounds. Therefore we use NeRF++ [39] as a baseline for this dataset. In Table 2 we present our results compared to NeRF++. Qualitative comparisons are presented in Fig. 5; since the units are unknown in this case we present relative improvement of Chamfer distance (in %) compared to NeRF. Also in this case, we improve NeRF reconstructions considerably, while being on-par in terms of the rendering quality (PSNR).

**Comparison to [38]** IDR [38] is the state of the art 3D surface reconstruction method using implicit representation. However, it suffers from two drawbacks: first, it requires object masks for training, which is a strong supervision signal. Second, since it sets the pixel color based only on the single point of intersection of the corresponding viewing ray, it is more pruned to local minima that Figure 6: IDR extraneous parts. sometimes appear in the form of extraneous surface parts. ## **4.2 Disentanglement of geometry and appearance**

We have tested the disentanglement of scenes to geometry (density) and appearance (radiance field) by switching the radiance fields of two trained scenes. For VolSDF we switched _Lψ_ . For NeRF [21] we note that the radiance field is computed as _Lψ_ ( _**z** ,_ _**v**_ ), where _Lψ_ is a fully connected network with one hidden layer (of width 128 and ReLU activation) and _**z**_ is a feature vector. We tested two versions of NeRF disentanglement: First, by switching the original radiance fields _Lψ_ of trained NeRF networks. Second, by switching the radiance fields of trained NeRF models with an identical radiance field model to ours, namely _Lψ_ ( _**x** ,_ _**n** ,_ _**v** ,_ _**z**_ ). As shown in Figure 7 both versions of NeRF fail to produce a correct disentanglement in these scenes, while VolSDF successfully switches the materials of the two objects. We attribute this to the specific inductive bias injected with the use of the density in equation 2.

## **5 Conclusions**

We introduce VolSDF, a volume rendering framework for implicit neural surfaces. We represent the volume density as a transformed version of the signed distance function to the learned surface geometry. This seemingly simple definition provides a useful inductive bias, allowing disentanglement of geometry (i.e., density) and radiance field, and improves the geometry approximation over previous neural volume rendering techniques. Furthermore, it allows to bound the opacity approximation error leading to high fidelity sampling of the volume rendering integral.

Some limitations of our method present interesting future research opportunities. First, although working well in practice, we do not have a proof of correctness for the sampling algorithm. We believe providing such a proof, or finding a version of this algorithm that has a proof would be a useful contribution. In general, we believe working with bounds in volume rendering could improve learning and disentanglement and push the field forward. Second, representing non-watertight manifolds and/or manifolds with boundaries, such as zero thickness surfaces, is not possible with an SDF. Generalizations such as multiple implicits and unsigned fields could be proven valuable. Third, our current formulation assumes homogeneous density; extending it to more general density models would allow representing a broader class of geometries. Fourth, now that high quality geometries can be learned in an unsupervised manner it will be interesting to learn dynamic geometries and shape spaces directly from collections of images. Lastly, although we don’t see immediate negative societal impact of our work, we do note that accurate geometry reconstruction from images can be used for malice purposes.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a well-motivated and technically sound advancement in neural volume rendering by rethinking the relationship between geometry and density representation. The core idea—modeling volume density as a function of a signed distance function (SDF), rather than treating geometry as a level set of an unconstrained density field—introduces a principled geometric prior that significantly improves both the fidelity of surface reconstruction and the stability of volume rendering. The formulation via Laplace’s CDF of an SDF is elegant and yields multiple benefits: it ensures a well-defined surface, enables error-bounded ray sampling for more accurate opacity integration, and facilitates unsupervised disentanglement of shape and appearance—contributions that are both theoretically grounded and empirically validated. The paper is clearly written, well-structured, and effectively positions VolSDF at the intersection of neural implicit surfaces and volume rendering, addressing key limitations in both domains. However, a major limitation lies in the reliance on the Eikonal regularization to enforce SDF properties, which may introduce optimization challenges and computational overhead; the paper would benefit from a more thorough discussion of this trade-off, including empirical validation of how closely the learned function adheres to a true SDF during training. Additionally, while comparisons to NeRF and IDR are provided, a more detailed ablation on the impact of each component—especially the error-bound-guided sampling—would strengthen the claims. Overall, the work represents a significant step toward more geometrically accurate neural rendering, with implications for both reconstruction quality and controllable synthesis.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the definition of density transformation with respect to SDF sign
comments.explanation: The use of −d_Ω(**x**) in Equation (2) may initially confuse readers because standard SDFs are negative inside the shape. Applying the increasing Laplace CDF Ψ_β to −d_Ω(**x**) means high values occur when d_Ω(**x**) is large and negative (i.e., deep inside the object), achieving the intended behavior. This is mathematically correct and consistent with the limit as β → 0 producing a scaled indicator of Ω. However, the negative sign is subtle and not explicitly justified in the text. Without clarification (e.g., that −d_Ω ensures high density in the interior), a reader might mistakenly expect Ψ_β(d_Ω(**x**)) for thresholding, which would invert the behavior. Given that SDF conventions vary and disentanglement relies on correct geometric interpretation, a brief note explaining the role of the negative sign would prevent misinterpretation.

While the subsequent equations (4) and (5) correctly define T(t) = exp(−∫σ) and O(t) = 1 − T(t), the phrase 'equivalently' is misleading in technical writing. This creates confusion: is S a fixed, predefined set or dynamically constructed? The initial presentation in Section 3.2 treats S as static, with no foreshadowing of the adaptive method in Section 3.4. Since T is used for coarse opacity estimation and later upsampled to inform S, the distinction is meaningful. To prevent misinterpretation, Section 3.2 should note that S will be adaptively sampled in practice based on learned opacity bounds, rather than being uniformly fixed.

comments.title: Confusion between learned β and sampling proxy β+ in convergence and error guarantees
comments.quote: We use the bisection method (with maximum of 10 iterations) to efficiently search for β⋆ and update β+ accordingly (Lines 6 and 7 in Algorithm 1). The algorithm runs iteratively until B_{T,β} ≤ ε or a maximal number of 5 iterations is reached.
comments.explanation: The algorithm maintains B_{T,β+} ≤ ε during sampling, with β+ > β acting as a smoothing proxy. However, the stopping condition uses B_{T,β} ≤ ε—i.e., the bound evaluated at the true, learned β. This conflation is problematic: B_{T,β} may exceed ε even if B_{T,β+} is satisfied, especially if β+ > β. The text claims β+ 'usually converges to β', but stopping based on B_{T,β} falsely suggests the true model’s error is bounded. Moreover, it's ambiguous whether β+ is used in computing σ during opacity estimation. If β+ ≠ β is used in σ, it introduces inconsistency with the learned model; if not, the bound may not reflect actual error. The roles of β and β+ need clearer separation in both notation and description.

comments.title: Inconsistent parameterization: α = β^−1 not reflected in error bound or parameter set θ

comments.explanation: The paper introduces α and β as learnable parameters, but states that α = β^−1 is used in practice. Consequently, α is not independently optimized, and θ correctly excludes it. However, the error bound B_{T,β} implicitly depends on α through the density derivative, yet does not incorporate α = β^−1 into its formulation. This disconnect between actual implementation and theoretical analysis may mislead readers into thinking the bound applies generically to any α, while in practice it is tied to β. Furthermore, while θ is correctly defined, the presentation gives the impression that β is learned freely—a concern when gradients depend on the α–β coupling. A comment linking the bound to the practical parameterization would improve clarity.

comments.title: Contradictory statements on α = β vs. α = β^{−1} in implementation

comments.explanation: There is a direct conflict between the paper's body and its summary: the summary states α = β in implementation, while Section 3.5 states α = β^{−1}. This is not a minor parametric choice—heavy-tailed behavior (β → 0) yields α → ∞ under α = β^{−1}, causing σ to diverge, which contradicts the stated limit σ → α **1**_Ω. Conversely, α = β keeps α bounded as β → 0. Given that both choices dramatically affect model scaling and learning dynamics, this contradiction undermines reproducibility and theoretical consistency. One of the two statements must be incorrect, and this requires urgent correction.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of minimal distance d* in derivative bound (Theorem 1)

comments.title: Flawed description of NeRF radiance field introduces misleading baseline
comments.quote: For NeRF [21] we note that the radiance field is computed as $ L_ψ ( z , v ) $, where $ L_ψ $ is a fully connected network with one hidden layer (of width 128 and ReLU activation) and $ z $ is a feature vector.
comments.explanation: This description inaccurately characterizes NeRF: the original formulation uses $ L_ψ(x, v) $, where x is position (post-positional encoding), not a global feature vector z. NeRF does not collapse spatial dependence into a single z; instead, it conditions radiance on per-sample x. Using z suggests a global feature model (e.g., NeRF++ or pixelNeRF), not vanilla NeRF. This misrepresentation weakens the disentanglement comparison, as poor disentanglement in the described variant may stem from architecture—not inherent to NeRF-style representation. For a fair comparison, baselines must reflect standard implementations.

comments.title: Underspecification in disentanglement evaluation procedure
comments.quote: We have tested the disentanglement of scenes to geometry (density) and appearance (radiance field) by switching the radiance fields of two trained scenes. For VolSDF we switched $ L_ψ $.
comments.explanation: The claim that disentanglement is demonstrated by 'switching $ L_ψ $' lacks crucial detail. The radiance field $ L_ψ $ depends on features $ z(x) $ and normal $ n(x) $, both derived from the geometry network $ f_φ $. To properly swap appearance, one must use the source $ L_ψ $ *and* feed it features computed from the target geometry. The text does not specify whether inputs to $ L_ψ $ were recomputed using the source or target geometry. If features from a different geometry were used, it would not be a clean ablation. Given that disentanglement is a central claim, the evaluation method must be fully specified for reproducibility.

comments.title: Unjustified assumption in radiance field input stability

comments.explanation: The use of $ n(t) = ∇_x d_Ω(x(t)) $ in the radiance field assumes $ d_Ω $ is differentiable and its gradient meaningful along the ray. However, learned SDFs may not satisfy $ ||∇d_Ω|| = 1 $ during training, especially early on. Without mention of gradient normalization, clamping, or Eikonal regularization timing, the numerical stability of $ n(t) $ as input to $ L_ψ $ is questionable. If $ n(t) $ is noisy or misaligned, it may entangle geometry with appearance rather than disentangle them. This critical implementation detail should be addressed given its role in the paper’s core claim.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the definition of density transformation with respect to SDF sign
comments.explanation: At first glance, the use of _−d_Ω(**x**) in the argument of Ψ_β raises a concern: standard signed distance functions are negative inside the shape (i.e., d_Ω(**x**) < 0 for **x** ∈ Ω) and positive outside. Applying the CDF of a Laplace distribution—which is increasing—to _−d_Ω(**x**) would then assign higher density values outside the object if d_Ω is negative inside. This seems to contradict the expectation that density should be high inside and low outside. However, upon closer inspection and in context of the sentence that follows—'as β approaches zero, the density σ converges to a scaled indicator function of Ω'—it becomes clear that the authors intend for high density inside Ω. This only holds if Ψ_β is large when its argument is large and positive, i.e., when _−d_Ω(**x**) is large positive ⇒ d_Ω(**x**) is large negative ⇒ **x** is deep inside Ω. That aligns with Ψ_β being the CDF of a zero-mean Laplace distribution, which increases with its argument, and thus _σ_(**x**) increases as d_Ω(**x**) becomes more negative. Therefore, although the expression is correct under this interpretation, the use of _−d_Ω_ instead of directly using a transformation that reflects 'interior distance' may confuse readers unfamiliar with the Laplace CDF's behavior. A brief clarification—e.g., noting that the negative sign ensures high density in the interior—would improve readability. While resolved via context, the initial ambiguity could mislead, especially given that SDF conventions vary across papers.

comments.title: Potential confusion between opacity O and transperancy T due to typo
comments.quote: the volume’s _opacity O_ , or equivalently, its _transperancy T_ ,
comments.explanation: The text states that opacity O is equivalent to 'transperancy T'. This is confusing because opacity and transparency are complements: O(t) = 1 − T(t), not equivalents. However, the subsequent equations (4) and (5) correctly define T(t) = exp(−∫σ) and O(t) = 1 − T(t), showing that the authors understand the distinction. The word 'equivalently' here seems to be used informally to mean 'related' or 'paired', which is not standard. In technical writing, claiming equivalence between complementary quantities is misleading. While this is likely just poor phrasing rather than a conceptual error, and the equations correct any misunderstanding, it momentarily undermines precision. 'Complementary' or 'reciprocal' would be more accurate than 'equivalently'. Given the context immediately resolves this, the issue is minor but still worth flagging for clarity.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for sample set indices in numerical integration
comments.explanation: The notation for the sample set S is presented as {si}[m] i=1 with s₁ = 0 and sₘ = M. However, in the discussion of the rectangle rule in Section 3.3, the samples are referred to as T = {ti}[n] i=1 with t₁ = 0 and tn = M. This is not inherently problematic—different variable names for different temporary sample sets are acceptable. However, later in Algorithm 1, the set T is upsampled and eventually used to estimate Ô, which is then used to generate S via inverse transform sampling. The distinction is meaningful: T is a coarse set used for opacity estimation, S is the final stratified sample. But in the initial definition in Section 3.2, S is treated as the final sample set used in quadrature (equation 8), and no mention is made of a secondary inference-time resampling step. This creates a dissonance: is S the uniformly or adaptively chosen sample set, or is it derived from T? Only after reading Section 3.4 and Algorithm 1 does it become clear that S is generated afresh from Ô⁻¹. Thus, the early use of S in equation (8) as if it were an arbitrary fixed sample set without foreshadowing its actual dynamic construction could mislead a reader into thinking sampling is static. The model is correct and the algorithm sound, but the notation assumes forward knowledge of Section 3.4. A clarifying note in Section 3.2 that 'S will later be chosen adaptively via opacity bounds' would resolve this ambiguity.

comments.title: Missing justification for monotonicity of exp(−R̂(t)) in Theorem 2 derivation
comments.quote: Finally, we can bound the opacity error for _t ∈_ [ _tk, tk_ +1] by noting that _E_[] ( _t_ ), and consequently also exp( _E_[] ( _t_ )) are monotonically increasing in _t_ , while exp( _−R_[] ( _t_ )) is monotonically decreasing in _t_ ,
comments.explanation: The argument relies on exp(−R̂(t)) being monotonically decreasing in t. R̂(t) is defined as the rectangle rule approximation to the integral of σ, which is non-negative. Hence R̂(t) is increasing in t, so −R̂(t) is decreasing, and since exp is increasing, exp(−R̂(t)) is decreasing—so this is correct. However, while mathematically sound, the claim is presented without explicit justification. In a paper emphasizing rigorous error bounds, it would be better to note that R̂(t) is increasing because σ ≥ 0 and the integral accumulates forward. The omission is minor given the simplicity of the result for readers familiar with integrals, but in the context of a theoretical contribution centered on bounds, even basic monotonicity should be briefly grounded. Still, this does not rise to a major flaw, as the statement is true and derivable from prior definitions.

The method section builds up the sampling and density model carefully, but without the training details—especially how the SDF is regularized and how radiance learns view-dependent effects—the full method cannot be reproduced. comments.title: Ambiguity in sign of SDF argument in density transformation
comments.explanation: At first glance, the use of _−d_Ω(_**x**_) in Equation (2) raised concern because the SDF, _d_Ω(_**x**_), is typically defined as negative inside the object and positive outside (i.e., _d_Ω(_**x**) < 0 for _**x** ∈ Ω_). The Laplace CDF Ψ_β is increasing, so applying it to _−d_Ω(_**x**_) means that inside the object (where _d_Ω is negative), _−d_Ω is positive, leading to high density — which aligns with the desired behavior. As _β → 0_, Ψ_β approaches a step function at 0, so Ψ_β(−d_Ω(_**x**)) → 1 when −d_Ω(_**x**) > 0, i.e., _d_Ω(_**x**) < 0, which corresponds exactly to _**x** ∈ Ω_, matching the claim that σ → α **1**_Ω. Thus, the negative sign ensures that high density is assigned to the interior (where SDF is negative), and the transformation smoothly approaches the indicator function. The authors are consistent with standard SDF conventions (as implied in the definition _d_Ω(**x**) = min_{**y**∈M} ||**x**−**y**|| ⋅ sign(dist)), and the use of −d_Ω is intentional and correct. However, the potential for confusion remains: a reader might expect the CDF to be applied directly to _d_Ω to threshold at zero, but that would assign high density to the exterior. The negative sign is therefore crucial and justified, but its necessity is not explicitly justified in the prose. A brief clarification (e.g., 'to ensure high density inside where SDF < 0') would prevent misinterpretation. Given that the math is correct and the behavior is verified in the limit, this is not an error but a point of ambiguity.

comments.title: Inconsistency in opacity error bound usage between Theorem 2 and Algorithm 1
comments.quote: **Theorem 2.** _For t ∈_ [0 _, M_ ] _, the error of the approximated opacity O_[ˆ] _can be bounded as follows:_ ... _BT ,β_ as a function of _T_ and _β_
comments.explanation: Theorem 2 presents a uniform bound _BT,β_ on the opacity approximation error that depends on the sampling _T_ and the shape parameter _β_. Lemma 2 then states that for a fixed number of samples, increasing _β_ reduces the error bound, enabling _BT,β ≤ ϵ_. However, in Algorithm 1, _β_+ is initialized to a value larger than the true _β_, and then reduced iteratively. This seems contradictory: increasing _β_ makes the density smoother (wider transition at boundary), which makes the derivative smaller and thus the error bound tighter — so using a larger _β_+ initially satisfies _BT,β+ ≤ ϵ_ more easily. Then, when updating, the goal is to decrease _β_+ toward the true _β_, which makes the density sharper and the error bound larger. The algorithm checks whether _BT,β > ϵ_ and upsamples if so. This logic aligns: use a conservative (larger) _β_+ to satisfy the bound at coarse sampling, then refine both sampling and _β_+ to approach the true density. The key potential confusion is that _β_ itself is a learnable parameter (controlling sharpness), but within the sampling algorithm, _β_+ is a temporary over-approximation used to ensure the opacity estimate is accurate during ray-marching. The paper does clarify that _β_+ > _β_ is chosen so that the bound holds, and bisection finds _β⋆_ such that _BT,β⋆ = ϵ_. However, the fact that _β_ is both a fixed (learnable) model parameter and used in the bound as a variable in Lemma 2 and Algorithm 1 could mislead: one might think _β_ is being changed during sampling, but in fact, during inference, _β_ is fixed, and _β_+ is an auxiliary variable for safe sampling. The explanation in Section 3.4 is sufficient upon close reading, but could be clearer. Since Algorithm 1 works with a fixed _β_ (from the model) and manipulates _β_+ as a proxy, the distinction is crucial. The method is sound, but the dual use of _β_ without explicit separation (e.g., calling it _β_smoothing_hint_) introduces ambiguity. However, given the context and the correct flow of logic (especially the bisection toward _β_), this is resolved in context.

comments.title: Undefined transparency function with missing integral in displayed equation
comments.quote:
The _transparency_ function of the volume along a ray _**x**_ , denoted _T_ , indicates, for each _t ≥_ 0, the probability a light particle succeeds traversing the segment [ _**c** ,_ _**x**_ ( _t_ )] without bouncing off,

3
comments.explanation: After the prose defining transparency _T_ as the survival probability along the ray, the expected continuation would be an equation defining _T(t) = exp(−∫_0^t σ(**x**(s)) ds)_. However, the next visible content is just the number “3”, which is clearly a page number or OCR artifact. The actual equation is missing in the provided passage, though it appears the authors intend to define _T_ via the integral of _σ_. Given the surrounding context and standard volume rendering conventions (and the later appearance of _τ = dO/dt_), the definition is recoverable and standard. The supplementary is referenced for derivation, suggesting the full form is elsewhere. In a complete paper, this equation would be present; its absence here is due to text extraction limits. Since the opacity _O_ is later defined as 1 − _T_, and _τ = dO/dt = σ exp(−∫σ)_, the functional form of _T_ can be inferred. No mathematical error occurs in the visible content, and the omission is due to OCR/page break, not author oversight. Thus, no real issue.

comments.title: Potential confusion between 'learnable β' and 'bound parameter β' in error analysis
comments.quote: Lemma 2. _Fix n >_ 0 _. For any ϵ >_ 0 _a sufficiently large β that satisfies ... will provide BT ,β ≤ ϵ._
comments.explanation: In Lemma 2, increasing β reduces the error bound — which aligns with the idea that smoother density (larger β) has smaller derivative, leading to smaller rectangle rule error. But β is also a learnable parameter in the model that controls the sharpness of the density transition. A reader might wonder: are we changing the model's β to satisfy the bound during training or inference? This would be problematic because β is meant to be learned from data, not set for convenience. However, the context in Section 3.4 resolves this: the algorithm uses a temporary β+ ≥ β to satisfy the error bound, then converges to the true β. Lemma 2 is used to initialize β+ large enough to make BT,β+ ≤ ϵ under coarse sampling. The true β remains fixed as a model parameter, but Lemma 2 is invoked not to change it, but to find a safe over-smoothed version for sampling stability. Therefore, Lemma 2 is applied not to the model's β directly, but to a hypothetical density with larger β for the purpose of bounding. This is a subtle but crucial distinction. The paper could clarify that the β in Lemma 2 refers to the parameter of a hypothetical density used for bound computation, not necessarily the trained one. But given that Algorithm 1 explicitly uses β+ > β, the intent is preserved. The logic holds, but the notation risks conflating two uses of β.

comments.title: Missing justification for rectangle rule error bound derivation
comments.quote: Details are in the supplementary. Equation 12 leads to the following opacity error bound, also proved in the supplementary:
comments.explanation: The paper states that the rectangle rule approximation error for the opacity integral is bounded using a derivative bound on the density (Theorem 1), leading to the error bound in Equation 12 and ultimately Theorem 2. However, the step from bounding σ′ to bounding the error in ∫σ is non-trivial, especially since the transparency T depends exponentially on the integral of σ, and the opacity O(t) = 1 − exp(−∫_0^t σ ds), so the error propagation is nonlinear. Standard error bounds for quadrature assume smoothness of the integrand, but here the opacity is a nonlinear transformation. The supplementary likely contains the full derivation, but within the main paper, the claim lacks intuitive justification. For instance, does the bound account for error accumulation in the exponential? The paper mentions that exp(−Rˆ(t)) and exp(Eˆ(t)) are monotonic, which suggests a Grönwall-type argument, but this is not elaborated. Given that bounding such an error in volume rendering with adaptive sampling is a key contribution, deferring all details to the supplementary makes the method less transparent. However, since the supplementary is cited and the paper is from NeurIPS (where supplementary is standard), and given that the empirical results in Figure 3 support the convergence, this is a mild shortcoming in exposition, not a flaw in the method.

comments.title: Inconsistent indexing in Theorem 1: interval [ti, ti+1] vs. [ti, ti−1]
comments.quote: The benefit in Theorem 1 is that it allows to bound the density’s derivative in each interval [ ti, ti−1 ] based only on the unsigned distance at the interval’s end points, |di|, |di+1|, and the density parameters α, β.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned that the interval [ti, ti−1] is referenced, which would imply a reversal of indices (since ti−1 < ti in an ordered sequence) and could suggest a typo. This contrasts with earlier and later use of [ti, ti+1] as the correct forward interval (e.g., 't ∈ [tk, tk+1]'). Checking the context, the surrounding definitions consistently use increasing index order, and Theorem 1 is stated for segment [ti, ti+1], not [ti, ti−1]. Furthermore, the use of |di|, |di+1| as endpoint unsigned distances only makes sense with consecutive indices in forward order. Therefore, the mention of [ti, ti−1] must be an OCR or typographical error. It contradicts the established notation and could mislead a reader about the interval indexing. Even assuming the intended interval is [ti−1, ti], it still doesn't align with the rest of the section, where intervals are indexed forward. The correct reference should be [ti, ti+1], consistent with T = {ti} and δi = ti+1 − ti.

comments.title: Missing expression for rectangle rule approximation in text
comments.quote:
Given some t ∈ (0, M], assume t ∈ [tk, tk+1], and apply the rectangle rule (i.e., left Riemann sum) to get the approximation:

4

is the rectangle rule approximation, and E(t) denotes the error in this approximation.
comments.explanation: The passage breaks into a numbered line break ('4') before showing the actual equation, which was likely meant to display the integral approximation. In the context, we expect to see something like R̃(t) = ∑_{i=1}^k σ_i δ_i as the piecewise constant (left-endpoint) approximation of ∫_0^t σ(x(s)) ds. The prose refers to 'the approximation' and 'E(t)' as the error, but the expression itself is missing — possibly due to OCR or formatting loss. While the concept is recoverable from context and the supplementary is referenced, a standalone reader cannot verify the exact form of the approximation or the error definition. This breaks the flow and risks ambiguity in what R̃(t) actually is (e.g., whether it's left, right, or midpoint). The field standard is left Riemann sum for such rendering approximations, so intent is clear. However, the absence of the expression remains a gap in completeness.

comments.title: Inconsistent subscript in opacity approximation Ô vs. Ō in bound derivation
comments.quote: Finally, we can bound the opacity error for t ∈ [tk, tk+1] by noting that Ê(t), and consequently also exp(Ê(t)) are monotonically increasing in t, while exp(−R̃(t)) is monotonically decreasing in t, and therefore
comments.explanation: The notation shifts from Ô (hat) as the approximated opacity to using Ê(t) and R̃(t) in the error decomposition. Earlier, the approximation of ∫σ ds is R̃(t), and E(t) is the error so that ∫_0^t σ ds = R̃(t) + E(t). Then T̃(t) = exp(−R̃(t)), and Ỗ(t) = 1 − T̃(t). But here, Ê(t) is used (with hat), which is not defined. Context suggests that Ê(t) should simply be E(t), the integral approximation error, which is already defined. The use of a hat may be an OCR artifact or inconsistent notation. If Ê(t) were an estimate of E(t), that would compound errors, but no such object is defined. The monotonicity argument relies on E(t) being positive and increasing due to the non-negativity of σ, which holds, but the shifted notation risks confusion. Since E(t) is already the error in the rectangle rule approximation of the integral, introducing a hatted version adds unnecessary ambiguity.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of d* as minimal distance between sets
comments.quote: The inset depicts the boundary of the open balls union Bi ∪ Bi+1, the interval [x(ti), x(ti+1)] and the bound is defined in terms of the minimal distance between these two sets, i.e., d*.. i..
comments.explanation: The phrase 'the minimal distance between these two sets' is ambiguous. Which two sets? Bi ∪ Bi+1 and the ray segment [x(ti), x(ti+1)]? Or Bi and Bi+1 themselves? The proof relies on geometric properties of signed distance functions, and typically such bounds involve the distance from the segment to the complement of the union of balls where the SDF is controlled. However, without a precise definition or reference to the supplementary (which isn't available), it’s unclear how d* is formally derived. The notation 'd*.. i..' suggests a subscripted d* with indices, likely d*_i, but is garbled. The OCR likely corrupted a subscript like d*_i or d_{∗,i}. While the general idea—that the derivative bound depends on local geometry of the SDF near the ray segment—is sound and standard in level-set methods, the lack of clarity in how d* is defined prevents verification of Theorem 1's applicability. This is a known construction in Eikonal equation analysis, but the omission of a formula or clearer description is problematic for reproducibility.

comments.title: Lemma 2 claims existence but lacks constructive condition
comments.quote: For any ϵ > 0 a sufficiently large β that satisfies _will provide BT,β ≤ ϵ._
comments.explanation: The statement of Lemma 2 is incomplete: it says 'a sufficiently large β that satisfies' but provides no condition or inequality afterward — the formula is missing, likely due to OCR truncation. In context, β controls the width of the transition in the density near the surface (σ(x) = α Ψ_β(−d_Ω(x))), so larger β makes the density smoother and the derivative smaller, hence reducing the error in the rectangle rule. Thus, the bound BT,β should decrease with β. But without the actual condition (e.g., β > f(ϵ, T)), the lemma is unusable. While Figure 3 and the algorithm description suggest that β+ is chosen to meet the error bound via a bisection, the omission of the required inequality undermines the theoretical grounding. The claim that large β reduces error makes intuitive sense (smoother functions are easier to integrate), but the lack of a formal condition makes Lemma 2 underspecified. This is critical because Algorithm 1 depends on it (Line 2).

comments.title: Algorithm 1 does not define how β+ is initialized despite relying on Lemma 2
comments.quote: Initialize β+ such that BT,β+ ≤ ϵ
comments.explanation: Line 2 of Algorithm 1 says to initialize β+ such that the error bound is satisfied, but no method is given for computing this value. Given that Lemma 2’s condition is missing (see previous issue), the algorithm lacks a specification for how β+ is chosen. While the text says 'according to Lemma 2', without the condition in Lemma 2, it’s impossible to reproduce this step. A practical implementation would require either a formula, a root-finding method, or a conservative upper bound on the derivative. The reliance on an undefined procedure makes the algorithm underspecified. Even though bisection is used later, the initial value of β+ must be large enough to satisfy the bound, which requires knowing how BT,β depends on β. This is particularly problematic because β is also a learnable parameter—so the algorithm balances between learning β and using a larger β+ for sampling, which affects gradients.

comments.title: Use of 'monotonically increasing' for exp(E(t)) assumes E(t) ≥ 0, which needs justification
comments.quote: Finally, we can bound the opacity error for t ∈ [tk, tk+1] by noting that Ê(t), and consequently also exp(Ê(t)) are monotonically increasing in t, while exp(−R̃(t)) is monotonically decreasing in t
comments.explanation: The argument that exp(E(t)) is monotonically increasing relies on E(t) being increasing, which in turn relies on the error in the rectangle rule being cumulative and non-negative. However, for a left Riemann sum of a non-negative function (σ ≥ 0), R̃(t) underestimates ∫σ ds, so E(t) = ∫σ ds − R̃(t) ≥ 0 and increases as t grows. While this is standard, the claim that E(t) is monotonically increasing is non-trivial — it depends on the monotonicity of both the true integral and the piecewise approximation. The true integral increases with t, and R̃(t) increases in steps, but E(t) could oscillate. However, within each interval [tk, tk+1], R̃(t) is constant, while ∫σ ds increases, so E(t) increases piecewise-linearly. After tk+1, R̃ jumps up, possibly reducing E(t). Therefore, E(t) is not globally monotonic. The argument likely applies per interval or relies on a bound that is monotonic in a piecewise sense. The justification is underspecified, and the claim as stated is questionable without additional constraints on σ or the sampling. This weakens the derivation of the uniform bound BT,β.

comments.title: Mismatch between stated m = 64 samples and T with n = 128 in upsampling
comments.quote: we use n = 128 in our implementation... Finally we return a fresh set of m = 64 samples
comments.explanation: The algorithm begins with T of size n = 128, then adds n = 128 more samples in each upsample step (Line 4). After several iterations, T becomes substantially larger (e.g., 128 → 256 → 384). Then, in Line 11, only m = 64 samples are drawn from Ô⁻¹ via inverse transform sampling. This raises a question: why invest in a large T to get a high-fidelity Ô, only to resample just 64 points? While this is efficient (using a detailed T to estimate Ô, then sampling sparsely from it), the justification for down-sampling is not given. Standard practice (e.g., in NeRF) uses ~64 coarse + 64 fine samples. Here, the fine set S (64 points) is derived from Ô, but the coarse set T (growing to 384+) is not reused. This could imply redundancy. It would be clearer if the algorithm reused some points or justified the reduction. While not an error, the inefficiency or lack of explanation could mislead readers about computational cost or sampling strategy. Given that T is already dense near boundaries, resampling 64 points may be sufficient, but the choice of m = 64 vs. the large T is unexplained.

comments.title: Inconsistency in parameterization: α = β⁻¹ not reflected in opacity error bound
comments.quote: In fact, in our implementation we make the choice α = β^{−1}. We denote by θ ∈ R^{[p]} the collection of all learnable parameters of the model, θ = (ϕ, ψ, β).
comments.explanation: The authors state that they set α = β⁻¹ in practice, which is a reasonable design choice to reduce the number of free parameters. However, this choice is not reflected in the opacity error bound B_{T,β}, which depends explicitly on α through the derivative bound in Theorem 1 and subsequently in the summation term of B_{T,β}. Given that α scales the density, and the rectangle rule error |E_\hat{}(t)| scales with α (via the integral ∫σ ds), setting α = β⁻¹ introduces a specific scaling dependence that should be incorporated into B_{T,β}. Yet the bound as defined does not substitute α = β⁻¹, potentially overstating generality. While this doesn't invalidate the bound, it creates a disconnect between the implementation and the theoretical presentation: the actual error control in experiments depends on this coupling, but the analysis treats α and β independently. A careful reader might be misled into thinking B_{T,β} is valid for arbitrary α, while in practice it's tied to β. The issue is resolvable from context, but the lack of comment on this coupling in the error analysis is a missed opportunity for clarity.

comments.title: Missing justification for monotonicity of exp(−R̂(t)) in derivation of uniform bound
comments.quote:
Finally, we can bound the opacity error for t ∈ [t_k, t_{k+1}] by noting that E_\hat{}(t), and consequently also exp(E_\hat{}(t)) are monotonically increasing in t, while exp(−R̂(t)) is monotonically decreasing in t, and therefore

|O(t) − Ô(t)| ≤ 1 − exp(−|E_\hat{}(t)|).
comments.explanation: The derivation of the uniform bound relies on the monotonicity of exp(−R̂(t)) to justify taking the maximum over intervals. R̂(t) is defined as the rectangle rule approximation, which is piecewise constant between samples and jumps at t_i. Thus, R̂(t) is non-decreasing, so exp(−R̂(t)) is non-increasing — this justifies the decreasing part. However, R̂(t) is piecewise constant, so exp(−R̂(t)) is flat within each [t_k, t_{k+1}), not strictly decreasing. The error term |O(t) − Ô(t)| depends on both exp(−R̂(t)) and exp(E_\hat{}(t)), and E_\hat{}(t) is increasing due to accumulation of integration error. But the combined behavior isn't trivially monotonic. The authors assert the bound without showing that the maximum occurs at interval endpoints — a key step. In practice, for convergence analysis, this bound is conservative and accepted in the field, so the claim likely holds, but the justification is underspecified. A reference to a standard result on error accumulation in rectangle rules for monotonic integrands would strengthen it.

comments.title: Mismatch between defined parameter collection and implementation choice in θ
comments.quote: We denote by θ ∈ R^{[p]} the collection of all learnable parameters of the model, θ = (ϕ, ψ, β).
comments.explanation: Earlier, the authors define both α and β as learnable scalar parameters, but then state that α = β⁻¹ in implementation. If α is not independently learnable, then θ should not include α as a separate parameter — and it doesn't. However, naming θ = (ϕ, ψ, β) and excluding α is correct *only if* α is strictly determined by β. But the presentation could mislead: one might assume β is learned freely while α is derived, yet in the model definition, both are said to be learnable. The resolution comes from the sentence 'in our implementation we make the choice α = β⁻¹', which implies α is not independently optimized. However, this is stated after θ is defined. A more careful reader might question whether α is optimized at all or how the constraint is enforced (e.g., via reparameterization or loss penalty). The issue is minor because the text resolves it shortly after, but reversing the order would improve clarity.

comments.title: Underspecification of sampling distribution in upsampling step
comments.quote: In order to reduce β_+ while keep B_{T,β_+} ≤ ϵ, n samples are added to T (Line 4 in Algorithm 1), where the number of points sampled from each interval is proportional to its current error bound, equation 14.
comments.explanation: Line 4 of Algorithm 1 refers to adding n samples to T with count per interval proportional to 'its current error bound' — referring to equation 14, which is the per-interval contribution to B_{T,β}. However, equation 14 is not an error per interval, but a cumulative sum up to k. B_{T,β} = max_k (1 − exp(−∑_{i=1}^k (α δ_i² / (2β)) ⋅ f(d_i^*))), so the per-k contribution isn't directly the term in the sum. The 'error bound' for interval i should be the additive contribution to the cumulative exponent — i.e., the term inside the sum for i. But this isn't specified. A reader cannot reproduce the sampling distribution without guessing that 'proportional to its current error bound' means 'proportional to the term corresponding to interval i in the sum ∑_i (α δ_i² / (2β)) ⋅ f(d_i^*)'. While plausible and likely correct, this is underspecified. Given that Algorithm 1 is central to the method, more precision in how the error guides sampling would help reproducibility.

comments.title: Potential confusion between β and β_+ in convergence description
comments.quote: We use the bisection method (with maximum of 10 iterations) to efficiently search for β⋆ and update β_+ accordingly (Lines 6 and 7 in Algorithm 1). The algorithm runs iteratively until B_{T,β} ≤ ϵ or a maximal number of 5 iterations is reached.
comments.explanation: The algorithm initializes β_+ > β and aims to reduce it toward β while ensuring B_{T,β_+} ≤ ϵ. The stopping condition is B_{T,β} ≤ ϵ — i.e., using the target β, not β_+. But during execution, the bound B_{T,β_+} is maintained, not B_{T,β}. The bisection finds β⋆ such that B_{T,β⋆} = ϵ, which allows decreasing β_+, but the final rendering uses β, not β_+. There is an implicit assumption that once T is refined enough, B_{T,β} ≤ ϵ. But this is only guaranteed if β_+ has been decreased to β and B_{T,β_+} ≤ ϵ — which may not happen in 5 iterations. The authors say 'β_+ usually converges to β (typically 85%)', implying it's not always exact. Yet the final sampling uses β. This suggests that β in B_{T,β} refers to the current β_+ during estimation, but the notation overloads β. A careful reader might be misled into thinking the algorithm ensures B_{T,β} ≤ ϵ with the true β, but it's only ensured for β_+, which may be larger. This doesn't break the method — as β_+ controls the bound — but the description conflates β and β_+, risking overstatement of error guarantees.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of $ \alpha $ and $ \beta $ in text vs. implementation
comments.quote: In fact, in our implementation we make the choice $ \alpha = \beta^{-1} $.
comments.explanation:
Initially, I was concerned about a contradiction between the definition of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in the PAPER SUMMARY and the passage. The summary states that $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{R}$ are scalar learnable parameters with $\alpha = \beta$ in implementation. However, the passage in Section 3.5 claims $\alpha = \beta^{-1}$. This is a direct conflict: one cannot have both $\alpha = \beta$ and $\alpha = \beta^{-1}$ unless $\beta = 1$, which is not generally true during training.

I checked whether this might be an OCR error (e.g., misreading $\beta$ as $\beta^{-1}$), but the LaTeX is clearly written as \beta^{-1}. Then I considered if the summary might be outdated or simplified, but the summary explicitly says 'in implementation' — indicating it is describing the actual code. The supplementary material is referenced for details, but since this is a core parametric choice, it should be self-consistent in the main text.

The issue remains: either the summary is wrong, or the body text is wrong. Given that the model’s behavior depends critically on the scale of $\alpha$ (which controls density magnitude) and $\beta$ (which controls SDF sharpness), setting $\alpha = \beta^{-1}$ introduces an inverse relationship that drastically changes the inductive bias compared to $\alpha = \beta$. This discrepancy undermines confidence in reproducibility and could affect disentanglement and convergence.

Moreover, Equation (2) defines $\sigma(\mathbf{x}) = \alpha \Psi_\beta(-d_\Omega(\mathbf{x}))$, where $\Psi_\beta$ is the Laplace CDF with scale $\beta$. Since the Laplace CDF has range [0,1], $\alpha$ acts as a maximum density. If $\alpha = \beta^{-1}$, then as $\beta \to 0$, $\alpha \to \infty$, making the density diverge — which contradicts the stated behavior that $\sigma \to \alpha \mathbf{1}_\Omega$ as $\beta \to 0$. For that limit to be meaningful, $\alpha$ should remain bounded. This suggests $\alpha = \beta$ is more plausible than $\alpha = \beta^{-1}$, especially with $\beta$ small.

comments.title: NeRF radiance field formulation conflicts with canonical definition
comments.quote: For NeRF [21] we note that the radiance field is computed as $ L_\psi ( \mathbf{z} , \mathbf{v} ) $, where $ L_\psi $ is a fully connected network with one hidden layer (of width 128 and ReLU activation) and $ \mathbf{z} $ is a feature vector.
comments.explanation:
At first glance, this seems like a reasonable summary. But given that the paper is positioning VolSDF against NeRF, accuracy in describing NeRF is critical. The cited NeRF paper (Mildenhall et al., NeurIPS 2020) defines the radiance field as $ L_\psi(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{v}) $, where $\mathbf{x}$ is the position (after positional encoding) and $\mathbf{v}$ is the viewing direction. The feature vector $\mathbf{z}$ is not directly used; instead, the coarse-to-fine sampling uses two networks: one for $\sigma$ and one for radiance, both conditioned on $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{v}$.

The description here suggests NeRF uses a feature vector $\mathbf{z}$ independent of $\mathbf{x}$, which is inaccurate. NeRF does pass a coarse sample encoding through a network to condition the fine network, but it does not reduce the spatial dependence to a global feature $\mathbf{z}$; position dependence is maintained. The use of $\mathbf{z}$ as a fixed feature per ray or image would be more characteristic of NeRF++ or other decomposed models, not vanilla NeRF.

This misstatement weakens the validity of the disentanglement comparison: if the authors are testing a non-standard version of NeRF (e.g., one that removes spatial dependence via a global $\mathbf{z}$), then the failure of disentanglement may be due to that architectural choice rather than a fundamental limitation of NeRF-style modeling.

While the authors attempt fairness by testing a second variant with normal dependence, the first version described is not representative of the original NeRF, potentially misleading readers about the baseline.

comments.title: Ambiguity in disentanglement evaluation methodology
comments.quote: We have tested the disentanglement of scenes to geometry (density) and appearance (radiance field) by switching the radiance fields of two trained scenes. For VolSDF we switched $ L_\psi $.
comments.explanation:
The claim of successful disentanglement hinges on the interpretation of what 'switching $ L_\psi $' means. Initially, I assumed this meant swapping the entire radiance MLP weights between two trained models, while keeping the geometry (SDF and $\mathbf{z}$) fixed. But $L_\psi$ in VolSDF depends on $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{n}, \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{z}$, where $\mathbf{n} = \nabla_\mathbf{x} d_\Omega(\mathbf{x})$ and $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{z}(\mathbf{x})$ are outputs of $\mathbf{f}_\phi(\mathbf{x})$ — i.e., they are derived from the geometry network.

Therefore, if both $\mathbf{n}$ and $\mathbf{z}$ depend on the geometry, and $L_\psi$ uses them as inputs, then simply switching $L_\psi$'s weights is not sufficient to swap appearance — one must also ensure that the inputs $\mathbf{z}(\mathbf{x})$ are evaluated using the source geometry, not the target. But the passage does not specify how the inputs to the swapped $L_\psi$ are computed.

For the disentanglement to be meaningful, one must render using: (1) geometry network $\mathbf{f}_{\phi_A}$, (2) radiance network $L_{\psi_B}$, and (3) inputs to $L_{\psi_B}$ computed from $\mathbf{f}_{\phi_A}(\mathbf{x})$ — ensuring that the appearance is modulated by the source geometry's features. If instead $\mathbf{z}(\mathbf{x})$ were taken from $\mathbf{f}_{\phi_B}$, that would be mixing geometries.

The text says only 'switched $L_\psi$', leaving this critical detail unspecified. Given that the visual result in Figure 7 appears plausible, the authors likely did it correctly, but the method is underspecified for reproduction. This is especially important because one of the claimed advantages of VolSDF is disentanglement — and without clarity on how it was tested, the claim is hard to validate.

comments.title: Unjustified assumption in radiance field dependency on $ \mathbf{n} $
comments.quote: Adding this dependency is motivated by the fact that BRDFs of common materials are often encoded with respect to the surface normal, facilitating disentanglement as done in surface rendering [38].
comments.explanation:
The motivation for including the normal $\mathbf{n}(t) = \nabla_\mathbf{x} d_\Omega(\mathbf{x}(t))$ in the radiance field $L_\psi$ is presented as standard and unproblematic. However, this assumes that the gradient $\nabla_\mathbf{x} d_\Omega$ is well-defined and meaningful along the ray, which is not guaranteed for a learned SDF.

Signed distance functions are only differentiable almost everywhere, and $\| \nabla d_\Omega \| = 1$ only where differentiable — a property enforced via the Eikonal loss. However, during training, especially early on, $\nabla d_\Omega$ may not be unit norm, and may even be noisy or undefined at some points.

The paper does not clarify whether $\mathbf{n}(t)$ is computed using automatic differentiation from the current $d_\Omega$, or whether it’s clamped or normalized. If used raw, it could introduce numerical instability or meaningless inputs to $L_\psi$. The disentanglement benefit relies on $\mathbf{n}$ being a clean geometric signal distinct from appearance — but if $\mathbf{n}$ is noisy or corrupted by appearance gradients, the disentanglement may be superficial.

While it's reasonable to assume the authors used $\nabla d_\Omega$ directly (standard practice), the lack of discussion on how $\mathbf{n}$ is computed, stabilized, or regularized is a gap, especially since it's central to the disentanglement claim.

comments.title: Missing clarification on $ \beta $ vs. $ \beta_+ $ in sampling algorithm
comments.quote: We use the bisection method (with maximum of 10 iterations) to efficiently search for $ \beta^\star $ and update $ \beta_+ $ accordingly (Lines 6 and 7 in Algorithm 1).
comments.explanation:
In Algorithm 1 and surrounding text, $\beta_+$ is used as a temporary slack variable to ensure the error bound $B_{T,\beta_+} \leq \epsilon$ during sampling refinement. While this is a clever way to allow coarse initial approximation, the text never clarifies whether $\beta_+$ is updated or used in the forward pass after sampling.

Specifically: is the density $\sigma(\mathbf{x}) = \alpha \Psi_\beta(-d_\Omega(\mathbf{x}))$ computed using the original $\beta$ or the adapted $\beta_+$? If $\beta_+$ differs from $\beta$, and is not fed back into the density function, then the sampling is based on a different model than the one being rendered — introducing an inconsistency between sampling distribution and actual density.

The paper states that the final $\hat{O}$ is estimated using $T$ and $\beta_+$, but $\hat{O}$ depends on $\sigma$, which depends on $\beta$ — unless $\beta_+$ is substituted for $\beta$ during opacity estimation. This is a key detail: if $\beta_+$ is only used for bounding purposes but not in computing $\sigma$, the bound may not hold. Conversely, if $\beta_+$ is used in $\sigma$, then the model effectively uses a different $\beta$ during rendering than the one being optimized — breaking gradient flow.

The most plausible interpretation is that $\beta_+$ is only used to compute $\hat{O}$ for sampling, but the actual volume rendering uses $\beta$. This would make sense, but introduces a distribution mismatch. The paper should clarify this point to avoid confusion.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The introduction and abstract for "Volume Rendering of Neural Implicit Surfaces" are highly effective, demonstrating both clear academic rigor and strong narrative motivation. The paper skillfully identifies a critical gap in contemporary neural volume rendering—specifically the limitations of unstructured density fields for geometric recovery—and proposes a compelling solution by re-parameterizing density through a signed distance function (SDF). The clarity of the technical contribution is commendable, particularly the description of how this representation introduces necessary inductive bias and enables analytical bounds on the opacity approximation error. However, a significant potential issue for a reader is the briefness of the technical "bridge" between the theoretical formulation and the practical optimization; while the conceptual shift is clear, the paper would benefit from explicitly addressing the potential instability of gradient propagation when training SDFs within an Eikonal framework, a known challenge in the field that the authors acknowledge only partially in the related work. Overall, the writing is precise, well-structured, and establishes a strong, logical foundation for the empirical results that follow.

comments.title: Unclear Definition of Bound in Lemma 1 and Lemma 2
comments.quote: Lemma 1. Fix beta > 0. For any epsilon > 0 a sufficient dense sampling T will provide B_{T, beta} < epsilon... Lemma 2. Fix n > 0. For any epsilon > 0 a sufficiently large beta will provide B_{T, beta} <= epsilon.
comments.explanation: The lemmas regarding the error bound $B_{T, \beta}$ are imprecise. Lemma 1 fails to mention that the required sampling density $T$ is dependent on the sharpness parameter $\beta$. Lemma 2 is potentially misstated or misleading; while larger $\beta$ values increase smoothness, it neglects the necessity of $\beta$ remaining small enough to maintain the structural fidelity of the geometry (i.e., the limit $\beta \to \infty$ results in zero density). The interplay between $\beta$, $n$ (sampling), and the error bound requires more explicit definition.

comments.title: Subscript Inconsistency and Incompleteness in Theorem 1
comments.quote: Bi = { x | || x - x(ti) || < |di|}, di = d_Omega(x(ti)).
comments.explanation: The definition of the geometric bound $B_i$ appears incomplete. It relies solely on the SDF value $d_i$ at the start of the interval, failing to account for the geometry or interval length across the segment $[t_i, t_{i+1}]$. This makes the Lipschitz constant bound derivation unclear to the reader.

comments.title: Lack of Formal Definition for Transparency Function
comments.quote: the probability a light particle succeeds traversing the segment [c, x(t)] without bouncing off
